example: Sensitivity analysis showed that the rank ordering of bank premiums is robust to changes in model specification.

topic: When the Bank Insures Itself: The Hidden Option in Deposit Insurance
definition: the risk of collapse of an entire financial system or entire market, due to the interconnectedness of its participants
example: When many banks fail simultaneously, systemic risk means the insurer cannot diversify away losses.

topic: When the Bank Insures Itself: The Hidden Option in Deposit Insurance
definition: an insurance premium that varies based on the measured riskiness of the insured entity
example: The option-based model produces a risk-adjusted premium for each bank based on its asset volatility.

topic: When the Bank Insures Itself: The Hidden Option in Deposit Insurance
definition: relating to assets or liabilities that do not appear on a company's balance sheet
example: Banks might shift risky assets into off-balance-sheet vehicles to avoid higher deposit insurance premiums.

topic: When the Bank Insures Itself: The Hidden Option in Deposit Insurance
definition: an adage stating that when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure
example: Using option-based pricing to set premiums could trigger Goodhart's law as banks change behavior to game the pricing model.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: the quality of being performed without conscious thought or effort
example: The automaticity of habitual behavior means you can drive a familiar route while your mind is elsewhere.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: a state of acting without conscious control, as if guided by an automatic system
example: Nearly half your daily actions run on autopilot, not conscious decision-making.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: a signal or trigger that initiates a specific behavior or response
example: A stable context provides the cue that automatically triggers the habitual action.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: the circumstances, environment, or setting in which a behavior occurs
example: Repeating a behavior in a consistent context strengthens the link between the two.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: causing harm or damage
example: Habits can be beneficial, like regular exercise, or detrimental, like smoking.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: firmly fixed or established, especially in the brain or memory
example: Old habits are hard to break because behavioral patterns become imprinted in neural pathways.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: increasing gradually by small amounts over time
example: Habit formation involves an incremental increase in the link between context and action.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: a route of connected neurons in the brain that transmits signals and strengthens with use
example: Repeated behaviors create and strengthen neural pathways that make the action automatic.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: the quality of continuing steadily despite difficulty or opposition; how long an individual engages in an activity
example: Motivation is characterized by direction, intensity, and persistence.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: to drive or push forward
example: Motivation is an internal state that propels individuals to engage in goal-directed behavior.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: occurring without conscious awareness or deliberate thought
example: A habit is a routine that tends to occur subconsciously.

topic: The 43% Rule: Why Most of Your Day Runs on Autopilot
definition: the ability to control one's own actions, emotions, or urges through conscious effort
example: Changing a habit is often framed as a willpower problem, but it is actually a context and repetition problem.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: a state of latent opposition between forces or parties
example: The tension between sales wanting high inventory and logistics wanting low inventory is the fundamental problem of supply chain management.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: a stock of goods held for future sale or use
example: The warehouse manager monitors inventory levels daily to avoid overstocking.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: the cost of storing and maintaining inventory, including warehousing, insurance, and obsolescence
example: Reducing holding costs is a key objective for the logistics department.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: a situation where inventory is exhausted and demand cannot be met
example: A stockout means lost sales and unhappy customers.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: relating to the underlying organization or framework of a system
example: The conflict between departments is structural, not a result of poor management.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: to make the best or most effective use of a resource or situation
example: Supply chain management aims to optimize the entire system, not just one department.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: to cause to occur at the same time or rate
example: A key goal of SCM is to synchronize supply with demand.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: the process of becoming outdated or no longer useful
example: Rapid product cycles increase the risk of inventory obsolescence.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: something that protects against shocks or fluctuations
example: Safety stock acts as a buffer against unexpected demand spikes.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: to optimize a part of a system at the expense of the whole
example: When each department sub-optimizes its own metrics, the total system suffers.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: the phenomenon where small fluctuations in demand cause increasingly larger fluctuations upstream in the supply chain
example: The bullwhip effect can be mitigated by better information sharing across the chain.

topic: The Hidden Tension in Every Supply Chain: Why Sales Wants More Inventory Than Logistics
definition: a conflict of interest that arises when one party (agent) acts on behalf of another (principal) but has different incentives
example: The sales-logistics tension resembles a principal-agent problem where the company is the principal and each department is an agent.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: An economic system where human attention is treated as a scarce commodity and the primary resource being allocated.
example: In the attention economy, companies compete not for customers' money but for their limited time and focus.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: The fundamental economic problem of having seemingly unlimited human wants in a world of limited resources.
example: Attention is the ultimate scarce resource in an age of information abundance.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: A basic good or resource that is interchangeable with others of the same type and is used in commerce.
example: In the attention economy, human attention becomes a commodity that platforms buy and sell.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: A mental shortcut that allows people to solve problems and make judgments quickly and efficiently.
example: Style serves as a heuristic for substance when we cannot evaluate every piece of information.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: Causing annoyance, anger, or another strong reaction, especially deliberately.
example: Provocative content is highly valuable in the attention economy because it captures immediate focus.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: Relating to or of the nature of dramatic or artistic performance; done for show rather than substance.
example: In an economy of attention, many public statements become performative acts designed to attract notice.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: Describing a market or situation where the top performer captures the vast majority of the rewards.
example: The attention economy has a winner-take-all dynamic where a few stylized voices dominate the conversation.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: A system where outputs of a process are fed back as inputs, causing the system to reinforce or regulate itself.
example: The feedback loop between style and attention means that successful styles are endlessly copied and amplified.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: A very large quantity of something; plentifulness.
example: We are drowning in an abundance of information, which makes attention the scarce resource.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: To distribute resources or duties for a particular purpose.
example: Economic theory helps us understand how to efficiently allocate scarce resources like attention.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: Unable to be touched or grasped; not having a physical presence.
example: Attention is an intangible resource, yet it drives the entire modern media economy.

topic: Why Style Is the Real Currency in the Attention Economy
definition: Forming or relating to an epoch; highly significant or momentous.
example: The shift from an economy of things to an economy of attention is an epochal transformation.
